g.data-narration: Welcome to Controlled Access Protocols - learn how networks coordinate access to shared channels without collisions.
Multiple devices sharing a communication channel creates collision risks. We need protocols to manage who transmits when.
Controlled access requires permission before transmission, eliminating collisions entirely unlike random access methods.
There are three main controlled access protocols: Reservation, Polling, and Token Passing. Each has unique characteristics.
g.text: Three Types of Controlled Access Protocols

g.data-narration: Reservation protocols divide time into two periods: reservation for claiming slots, and data transmission for actual sending.
g.text: How it works: Stations reserve slots during reservation period, then transmit in their assigned data slots.

g.data-narration: Polling uses a master-slave architecture where a controller systematically asks each station if it has data to send.
Polling uses two commands: SELECT to send data TO a secondary, and POLL to request data FROM a secondary.
g.text: "I have data to send to you"
Response: Secondary sends data frame if available, or NULL frame if no data to send.

g.data-narration: Token passing uses a special control frame called a token that circulates among stations, granting exclusive transmission rights.
g.text: Key: Only the station holding the token can transmit. Token circulates in logical ring (A→B→C→D→A).

g.data-narration: In token ring, when a station receives the token and has data, it transforms the token into a data frame and sends it.
g.text: IEEE 802.5 = Token Ring | IEEE 802.4 = Token Bus

g.data-narration: The token frame is a simple 3-byte control frame. Data frames include source, destination, and control information.
g.text: DA=Destination Addr, SA=Source Addr, FCS=Frame Check Sequence

g.data-narration: Token loss is a critical failure mode. Networks implement token recovery procedures using beaconing and claim token processes.
Compare controlled access methods: token passing is most efficient under heavy load, polling is simplest, reservation works for predictable traffic.
Example: In a 4-station ring with A having 3 frames, B having 1 frame, C and D having none. Watch the token cycle.
g.text: A still has 2 frames for next cycle

g.data-narration: Example calculation: 5 stations, 1ms polling overhead, 2ms data transmission, 0.3 probability. Average time = 8ms.
g.text: Given: n=5 stations, Polling overhead=1ms, Data time=2ms, Probability=0.3

g.data-narration: Example: 100 reservation slots of 0.1ms each, 10 stations send data. Efficiency = 5ms/12ms = 41.67%.
g.text: Given: 100 slots × 0.1ms = 10ms reservation, 2ms data period, 10 active stations

g.data-narration: Key points: Controlled access eliminates collisions through permission. Three types: Reservation, Polling, Token Passing.
g.text: Token circulates, only holder can transmit. Best efficiency under heavy load.

g.data-narration: Exam tips: Remember SELECT vs POLL, token recovery procedures, and practice numerical problems on polling and reservation.
Real-world applications: IBM Token Ring networks, early Ethernet variants, and certain wireless networks use controlled access.
